title: Comparative Adjectives and Numbers in Arabic
summary: The session focused on using comparative adjectives like '_ahsanu', 'at-ta'lu', and 'arkhasu' to express comparisons between nouns. The teacher also covered specific rules for the sisters of 'inna' (e.g., 'lakinna', 'ka'anna') and discussed numbers from 11 to 20, including gender agreement and ordinal forms.
key_points: Comparative adjectives such as '_ahsanu' (better) are formed by adding '-aw' or similar endings to the root. For example, 'a_hsanu min_' means 'better than'.
Use the structure '(pronoun + ) _adjective_ min _noun_ _case_.' when comparing. Example: 'Antum at-tawwilon minil-maalimin' (You are the richest among the teachers).
Sisters of 'inna', like 'ka'anna', trigger a fatha on the mubtada and retain the khabar's case. Example: 'Ka'anna Hu a_khuka' (It seems he is your brother).
Numbers 11-19 use singular ma’adud with opposite gender for adjectives, while 20+ follow the same rules as cardinal numbers. Ordinal forms add endings like '-iin' or 'at-taa'.
questions: [00:03:00] Q: How to combine min and hua? — A: It becomes 'min hu', creating a connected pronoun (dhamir muttasil).
[00:21:00] Q: Why is it 'kasla' for females instead of 'kaslan'? — A: Feminine forms drop the nun and add appropriate endings like '-a'.
